summary: The Manus scheduled task interface demonstrates a clear conversational flow with progressive disclosure of task creation steps, but suffers from several accessibility concerns including low-contrast text, small icon-only buttons without labels, and unclear interactive affordances. The information hierarchy within the chat is generally well-structured, though the density of nested cards and multiple expandable sections can increase cognitive load.

issues.title: Icon-only buttons lack accessible text labels
issues.location: Edit (pencil) icon on the Daily Trending Topics Summary card, copy and overflow (…) icons on the scheduled task card
issues.problem: Multiple interactive icons (pencil edit, clipboard copy, three-dot overflow menu) have no visible text labels or apparent aria-labels. Screen reader users cannot determine the purpose of these controls, and sighted users may also be uncertain of their function.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 1.1.1 requires all non-text content serving a function to have a text alternative. Without labels, assistive technology users are completely blocked from understanding these controls.
issues.recommendation: Add visually hidden aria-label or sr-only text to each icon button (e.g., 'Edit task description', 'Copy task', 'More options'). Consider adding tooltip text on hover for sighted users as well.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.1.1 Non-text Content (A): all functional non-text content must have a text alternative

issues.title: Low contrast on secondary and descriptive text
issues.location: 'Scheduled task' label under daily_trending_topics_summary, and the truncated description text in the Daily Trending Topics Summary card
issues.problem: The light gray text used for secondary descriptions (e.g., 'Scheduled task', truncated summary text) appears to have insufficient contrast against the white/light background, likely falling below the 4.5:1 ratio required for normal-sized text.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 1.4.3 mandates a minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text. Users with low vision or those viewing in bright environments will struggle to read these labels, reducing task comprehension.
issues.recommendation: Increase the text color to at least #767676 on white backgrounds to meet the 4.5:1 minimum ratio. Test all gray text values with a contrast checker tool like WebAIM's.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.4.3 Contrast Minimum (AA): normal text requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio

issues.title: Dismiss and Accept buttons have small touch targets
issues.location: Dismiss and Accept action buttons on the Daily Trending Topics Summary card
issues.problem: The 'Dismiss' and 'Accept' buttons appear compact with minimal padding, likely falling below the 24x24px minimum target size. On touch devices, users may accidentally tap the wrong action, especially given that Dismiss is destructive.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 2.5.8 requires a minimum target size of 24x24 CSS pixels. Small targets increase error rates per Fitts' Law, and placing a destructive action (Dismiss) adjacent to a confirmatory one (Accept) with small targets amplifies the risk.
issues.recommendation: Increase the clickable area of both buttons to at least 44x44px (WCAG AAA recommendation) or minimum 24x24px. Add more internal padding and increase spacing between the two buttons to reduce accidental taps.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 2.5.8 Target Size Minimum (AA): interactive targets must be at least 24x24 CSS pixels

issues.title: Destructive 'Dismiss' action lacks confirmation safeguard
issues.location: Dismiss button (× Dismiss) on the task proposal card
issues.problem: The Dismiss button appears to immediately discard the proposed scheduled task with no confirmation dialog. Users could accidentally dismiss a complex task setup with a single click, requiring them to re-enter the entire prompt.
issues.critical_reason: Norman's Forcing Function principle states that irreversible or costly actions should include confirmation steps to prevent errors. Loss aversion (Kahneman) means losing a configured task feels disproportionately negative.
issues.recommendation: Add a confirmation step before dismissing the task proposal, such as a brief 'Are you sure?' inline confirmation or an undo toast notification that persists for 5-10 seconds after dismissal.
issues.reference: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Forcing Function: prevent irreversible actions with confirmation

issues.title: Unclear visual hierarchy between task steps and final confirmation
issues.location: The sequence of expandable sections: Daily Trending Topics Summary card → Create scheduled task → Confirm task scheduling
issues.problem: Three sequential expandable sections plus a floating bottom confirmation bar create a dense vertical stack where the relationship and flow between steps is not immediately clear. The green checkmarks on some steps but not others adds ambiguity about which steps are complete vs. pending.
issues.critical_reason: Gestalt's Law of Proximity and similarity suggests that items grouped closely with similar visual treatment are perceived as related. The uniform card styling makes it hard to distinguish completed steps from active ones, increasing cognitive load.
issues.recommendation: Differentiate completed steps with a muted/collapsed visual style and highlight the currently active step. Use a vertical stepper or progress indicator to make the sequential relationship explicit.

issues.title: Truncated task description hides critical information
issues.location: Daily Trending Topics Summary card description text ending in '…'
issues.problem: The task description is truncated with an ellipsis, hiding potentially important details about what was proposed. Users must mentally compare the truncated preview to what they originally typed to verify accuracy before accepting.
issues.critical_reason: Don't Make Me Think (Krug) emphasizes that users scan rather than read — truncated content forces users to take extra action to verify completeness, increasing interaction cost and the chance of accepting an incorrect task definition.
issues.recommendation: Show the full task description by default in the review card, or provide a clear 'Show more' link. At minimum, ensure the visible portion includes all actionable parameters (time, platforms, format).
issues.reference: Don't Make Me Think (Krug) — Billboard Design 101: users scan, not read

issues.title: Left sidebar icons lack text labels
issues.location: Left navigation sidebar with icon-only navigation items
issues.problem: The left sidebar contains approximately 7 icon-only navigation items with no visible text labels or tooltips apparent. Users unfamiliar with the icon meanings must guess their function, and screen reader users may lack context if aria-labels are not provided.
issues.critical_reason: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) states that signifiers must clearly indicate where and how to act. Icon-only navigation without labels increases the gulf of execution, especially for new users who haven't learned the icon vocabulary.
issues.recommendation: Add text labels beside or below each icon, or at minimum provide tooltip labels on hover and aria-label attributes for accessibility. Consider an expandable sidebar that shows labels when space permits.
issues.reference: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Signifier: visible cues must indicate where to act

issues.title: Floating avatar/action button overlaps content
issues.location: Bottom-left floating element near the Manus avatar icon at approximately y:77%
issues.problem: A floating element with the Manus icon and a monitor/screen icon overlaps the bottom of the chat content area. This obscures part of the conversation text and creates visual clutter that competes with the primary content.
issues.critical_reason: Universal Principles of Design's Signal-to-Noise Ratio principle states that maximizing relevant information and minimizing clutter improves comprehension. Overlapping UI elements reduce readability and can obscure actionable content.
issues.recommendation: Reposition the floating element to avoid overlapping chat content, or dock it in a fixed location outside the scrollable content area. Ensure it does not cover any interactive elements when scrolled.
issues.reference: Universal Principles of Design — Signal-to-Noise Ratio: maximise relevant information, minimise clutter

issues.title: No visible focus indicators on interactive elements
issues.location: All interactive elements: Dismiss/Accept buttons, expand/collapse chevrons, sidebar icons, text input field
issues.problem: There are no visible keyboard focus indicators on any interactive elements in the screenshot. Keyboard-only users cannot determine which element is currently focused, making navigation impossible without a mouse.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 2.4.7 requires that keyboard focus indicators are visible. Without them, keyboard and switch-device users cannot navigate the interface, creating a complete barrier to access for these user groups.
issues.recommendation: Implement a visible focus ring (e.g., 2px solid outline with offset) on all interactive elements including buttons, links, expand/collapse controls, and the input field. Ensure the focus indicator has at least 3:1 contrast against adjacent colors.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 2.4.7 Focus Visible (AA): keyboard focus indicator must be visible

issues.title: Step pagination indicator '2 / 2' lacks context
issues.location: '2 / 2' indicator next to 'Confirm task scheduling to user' at the bottom
issues.problem: The '2 / 2' pagination text at the bottom-right of the confirmation step provides no context about what is being counted (steps? messages? confirmations?). Users may not understand what this number represents or how to navigate between items.
issues.critical_reason: Nielsen's Visibility of System Status heuristic requires that the system clearly communicates what is happening. Ambiguous status indicators create uncertainty and reduce user confidence in the system's state.
issues.recommendation: Add contextual labeling such as 'Step 2 of 2' or 'Message 2 of 2'. If navigable, ensure the up/down chevrons have aria-labels explaining their function (e.g., 'Previous message', 'Next message').
issues.reference: Universal Principles of Design — Visibility: system status and available actions must be visible
